food_subgroup: Processed fish products — spiced fish paste cake, grilled in banana leaf

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for otah/otak-otak. FOODON_03302051 (fish cake food product) is the closest parent category covering spiced fish paste products.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Fish cake) is the closest parent. No specific otak-otak or otah code exists in FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for otah or otak-otak. Fish cake concepts in SNOMED CT are not sufficiently specific for this preparation.

main_ingredients: spice paste (lemongrass, galangal, chilli, turmeric, shallots, garlic)

serving.natural_language_portion: One piece of otah typically weighs approximately 50 g and is served individually wrapped in a banana leaf, grilled until fragrant.

nutrient_source.title: Otak-otak (fish), grilled — HPB Singapore Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo lists otak-otak (fish, grilled) as a distinct entry. Values below are drawn from that record. Otah is the Singaporean colloquial name for otak-otak; the food is identical.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — fish cake / protein-rich spiced fish product analogue
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for otah/otak-otak in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 28 (low) by analogy with fish cake and high-protein, moderate-fat spiced fish products reported in Atkinson et al. 2021 (Diabetes Care). Glycemic load per 50 g serving = (28 × 5.4 × 0.50) / 100 = 0.76, rounded to 0.8. The low GI is consistent with the high protein and fat content of this food, which attenuates glycemic response.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content — approximately 420 mg per 100 g; one piece (50 g) contributes ~210 mg sodium
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit otah to one piece per meal occasion and avoid pairing with other high-sodium condiments or dishes, as cumulative sodium intake can elevate blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~28) and low glycemic load per serving (~0.8) due to high protein and fat content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Otah has a low glycemic index and low glycemic load per piece, making it a suitable protein-rich snack or side dish for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in moderation as part of a balanced meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from coconut milk (~5.2 g per 100 g) and moderate sodium; however, fish provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be mindful of the saturated fat contributed by coconut milk in otah; limiting to one piece and choosing versions made with leaner fish can help manage saturated fat intake while still benefiting from the omega-3 content of the fish.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Otah (also known as otak-otak) is a traditional Singaporean and Malay spiced fish paste cake, typically made from minced or blended fish — most commonly Spanish mackerel (ikan tenggiri) — mixed with a rich spice paste of lemongrass, galangal, chilli, turmeric, shallots, garlic, and coconut milk, then bound with egg. The mixture is wrapped in banana leaves and grilled over charcoal or an open flame until the exterior is fragrant and slightly charred, imparting a distinctive smoky aroma. The texture is soft, moist, and custard-like on the inside, with a firm outer layer. The flavour profile is savoury, mildly spicy, and aromatic, with the sweetness of coconut milk balancing the heat of the chilli. Otah is commonly sold as a street food snack or hawker centre side dish in Singapore and Malaysia, often served alongside nasi lemak, bread, or as a standalone snack. Nutritionally, otah is a moderate-calorie, high-protein food (~12.8 g protein per 100 g) with a notable fat contribution from coconut milk (~9.6 g fat per 100 g, of which ~5.2 g is saturated). Sodium content is moderate to high (~420 mg per 100 g), which is relevant for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic index is low (~28), making it suitable for blood glucose management. One piece weighs approximately 50 g and provides roughly 80 kcal.
common_user_queries: How many calories in one piece of otah?
What fish is used in otah?
What is the GI of otah?

confidence_reason: HPB Singapore Foodo lists otak-otak (fish, grilled) as a distinct named entry, which is the same food as otah. Nutrient values are drawn directly from this primary regional source. GI is estimated by analogy from an approved GI source (University of Sydney / Atkinson et al. 2021) as no direct measurement exists, which slightly limits confidence for the GI component only; overall record confidence remains high.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo source database for this entry. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo source database for this entry. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo source database for this entry. Trans fat is 0.0; confirmed zero per HPB source — otah does not contain industrially produced trans fats. GI is an analogous estimate (28, low) based on fish cake and high-protein spiced fish product analogues from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 (Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for otah/otak-otak was found. Serving size of 50 g per piece is consistent with typical hawker centre portion for one piece of otah as sold at Qi Ji and similar outlets. The food_name is normalised to 'Otah' (canonical English form); the input name 'Otah (Per Piece)' is retained as an alias. Halal status is based on the typical preparation of otah using fish, egg, and plant-based spices with no pork or alcohol; individual vendor halal certification should be verified independently.
